Q1: I had a very structured routine that I followed religiously. Every morning I would wake up at 6 AM, do some mindfulness exercises, and then dedicate about four to five hours to studying before my other responsibilities. I created color-coded schedules and visual planners that mapped out exactly when I would tackle each course module. The routine felt comfortable and safe to me, even though I realize now that maybe I was going through the motions more than actually absorbing the material.
Q2: I was extremely regular with my study times. I blocked out the same hours every single day, Monday through Sunday, and I stuck to that schedule no matter what. Deadlines didn't really change my pattern because I had already built in time for assignments well in advance. The consistency made me feel in control, which was important because honestly, the content of the exams terrified me even though I kept showing up.
Q3: I logged into the LMS almost automatically as part of my morning routine. It was like checking email or brushing my teeth - just something I did every day without really thinking about it. Sometimes I would log in multiple times throughout the day just to make sure I hadn't missed any announcements. The fear of missing something important drove a lot of my checking behavior.
Q4: Yes, using the LMS became completely automatic for me within the first two weeks. I set specific times to check it and created visual cues around my workspace to remind me. It became so ingrained that I would feel anxious if I didn't log in by a certain time each day. The routine was comforting even when I was scared about my actual understanding of the material.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm a visual learner. I watched them multiple times, sometimes over 250 minutes worth, pausing to take detailed notes with diagrams and flowcharts. The discussion boards were also helpful because seeing how other students interpreted concepts gave me different visual perspectives on the material. I really appreciated when instructors used graphics and charts in their presentations.
Q6: I tended to skim through the dense reading materials and text-heavy resources. My brain just doesn't process walls of text as efficiently as visual content. I also didn't engage much with the live sessions because my attendance was complicated by my schedule and honestly, the real-time pressure made me anxious. I know I should have attended more, but I convinced myself I could catch up with recordings.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear visual structure and knew exactly what was expected of me. I needed to understand how completing it fit into my overall schedule and grade. If something had clear checkboxes and progress indicators, I was much more likely to engage fully. The sense of completion and ticking things off my list was genuinely satisfying.
Q8: Required activities always took priority in my schedule and I completed them with meticulous attention to deadlines. Optional activities felt like nice-to-haves that I would get to if I had extra time, which I rarely did given how many courses I was juggling. I sometimes felt guilty about skipping optional content, but I had to be strategic with 20 online courses demanding my attention. The optional stuff often got pushed aside even though it might have helped me understand concepts better for exams.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline. I would download lecture slides and print them out so I could annotate them with colored pens and highlighters. I created elaborate visual study guides, mind maps, and flashcards that I reviewed away from my computer. I also used external YouTube videos and educational apps that explained concepts in more visual ways when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened with printed materials spread across my desk while I drew connections between concepts. I would spend hours reorganizing information into visual formats that made sense to me, none of which showed up anywhere in the LMS. My peer study sessions with classmates happened over video calls outside the platform too. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual study effort.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I would watch a video once in the LMS to get the general idea, then download the slides and any supplementary materials. After that, all my real studying happened offline with those downloaded resources. I probably spent three times as much time studying offline as the LMS would ever show. It was just more comfortable for me to work with physical materials I could spread out and visualize.
Q12: The hardest moments came when exam time approached. Even though I had been diligently completing assignments and watching videos, I would suddenly feel this wave of fear about whether I actually understood anything. The mindfulness exercises helped me manage the panic, but there were definitely times when I felt paralyzed by the gap between my routine completion and my actual comprehension. Juggling 20 courses made it impossible to give each one the deep attention it needed.
Q13: The sheer volume of 20 courses created constant cognitive overload. I was so focused on maintaining my routine and checking boxes that I sometimes lost sight of actually learning deeply. Fatigue definitely set in, especially toward the end of the semester, and I found myself going through motions on the LMS without really engaging mentally. My stress was high even though I had all these systems in place to manage it.
Q14: This happened more often than I'd like to admit. I would have videos playing while my mind wandered to my other courses or responsibilities. Sometimes I would scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was being active. The LMS would show I was engaged, but I was really just present, not processing. It's scary to acknowledge that now.
Q15: I avoided the practice exams and self-assessment quizzes because they terrified me. I knew they were important for preparing for the actual exams, but facing evidence that I might not understand the material was too anxiety-inducing. I told myself I would get to them once I felt more prepared, but that moment never came. It's probably why my exam score was so low despite completing most assignments.
Q16: Deadlines were the backbone of my entire system. I would work backward from each deadline and schedule exactly when I needed to complete each component. This meant I was always submitting on time and the LMS showed consistent activity around due dates. However, I realize now that this deadline focus made me prioritize completion over comprehension, which hurt me on exams that required deeper understanding.
Q17: Feedback was something I both craved and feared. When I got positive feedback on assignments, it reinforced my routine and made me feel like I was on the right track. Negative or constructive feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral of self-doubt. I would engage more intensely after receiving feedback, trying to incorporate suggestions, but the fear of criticism also made me hesitant to participate in discussions sometimes.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines worked well for my routine-oriented approach. I could map everything out visually and know exactly what was expected each week. However, the structure didn't really encourage the kind of deep engagement that would have prepared me for exams. It was easy to complete tasks without necessarily mastering concepts, and the structure almost enabled my surface-level approach.
Q19: The visual elements like video lectures and infographics made engagement much easier for me. Clear navigation and progress tracking also helped me stay on top of things. What made it harder was when content was primarily text-based or when exam preparation resources were buried or unclear. The disconnect between assignment style and exam style also made it difficult to know if I was truly prepared.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I was highly active - logging in frequently, watching videos, participating in discussions - but my actual submitted work and especially my exam performance didn't match that activity level. For me, it was about the comfort of routine and the fear of actually testing my knowledge. High activity can be a way of feeling productive without confronting whether you're actually learning.
Q21: I can see how that would work for some students who are just more efficient or who learn better through methods that don't show up in the LMS. They might read something once, understand it deeply, and not need to rewatch or revisit. That wasn't my experience though - I needed lots of repetition and visual processing, but even that didn't translate to exam success. Different learning approaches just show up differently in the data.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, time spent, and completion rates. They capture the routine and the consistency. But they completely miss the quality of engagement - whether someone is actually processing information or just going through motions. They can't see the offline studying, the anxiety behind the clicks, or the difference between watching a video attentively versus having it play in the background.
Q23: To me, being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just completing tasks. It means feeling curious and connected to what you're learning, not scared of it. Real engagement would have meant I could explain concepts to someone else, not just recognize them in multiple choice questions. I was engaged with the routine of the course, but I'm not sure I was truly engaged with the learning itself.
Q24: The LMS would never show how scared I was throughout this course, or how much the mindfulness exercises helped me just get through each day. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent creating visual study materials offline, or the peer study sessions that happened outside the platform. Most importantly, it wouldn't show the disconnect between my diligent routine and my actual understanding - the gap between looking like an engaged student and actually being one.
